channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: The Legend of Vox Machina S4 Review - A Family Divided
item.content:encoded:
The Legend of Vox Machina Season 3 managed to leave us in a strange place. The Chroma Conclave had been taken care of, and love had been found among much of the party, but there was also sadness in the departure of Scanlan and the ominous tease of a cult attempting to bring a new evil into the world of Exandria. A year later, the world is very different for our heroes, separated and each pursuing their own exploits. How does Season 4 bring them back together to face their greatest foe yet?

We pick up with each of the party in their own sections of Tal'dorei. Keyleth and Vax are off finishing her Aramente, Percy and Vex are trying to grow accustomed to the life of a Lord and Lady, and Grog and Pike are just kinda off adventuring. It was a neat introduction to give the audience bite-sized updates to each character's stories, while also pairing them with their most trusted to let them voice their insecurities.

If you're interested in hearing more about Season 4 of The Legend of Vox Machina from the full cast check out our interview with them where we discuss picking plotlines, how they maintain levity, and how inviting talent to join has gotten easier over the years.

Meet Taryon Darrington, Newest Member Of Vox Machina, Whether They Like It Or Not

Each grouping is trying to remain independent, or not pull the other party members into shenanigans related to the Ziggaurat orb or undead/zombified clerics, but the tethers that link Vox Machina together begin to tighten with the boisterous introduction of Taryon Darrington, played by Wayne Brady.

Tary, a fan of Vox Machina, seeks to join the party to help make a name for himself, but his prompts for adventure quickly get each group of the party into larger and larger dangers. This season did a fantastic job of taking advantage of what just wouldn't be possible at the D&D table, letting stories happen miles apart and letting Tary travel from one group of the party to the other. In many ways, Tary plays the everyman for the viewer as we drift between the multiple plot threads.

The introduction of Tary also brings levity to a lot of the characters' performances. Pike is worrying about the Everlight, Vax is nursing his cursed wounds, and Percy is feeling like he's never able to do enough to protect his friends. Among the rest of the party, there's a lot of seriousness going on and character arcs that have somewhere settled or are reaching their finality. With Tary in the mix, these characters are able to show off some snark and wit, and before too long, welcome the lovable goofball into the team.

I was a big fan of the levity that Tary brought and how his brash approach to pretty much everything he did forced the characters to confront their fears. As each smaller group of Vox Machina continued to try to isolate themselves, or did not want to share their worries or fears with the other party members, I couldn't stop myself from thinking, "You know, a lot of the problems you're all facing could be solved if you just talked with one another."

Once we're caught up with each of the characters and the seeds of adventure have been sewn, the adventure kicks off, drawing the group back together and into deep trouble as it's once again their job to save the world from a cult trying to bring back The Whispered One. This culminates in some epic encounters and a tease that will lead us into the final season.

Unlike the gap between Season 2 and Season 3, this one isn't able to pick up in the middle of the action. The breakneck pace of Season 3 was something that I understood, but also lamented as it didn't let us sit in moments as much. Season 4 does not have that problem; instead slowly teases and builds up the mystery for the viewer, delivering on massive reveals and set-piece moments.

Vox Machina Still Gets Up To Some Zany Hijinks

While the primary plot thread of The Whispered One is ever-present, there's enough breathing room in a few of the side-quest adventures of the season to still get some classic Vox Machina slapstick action going on. Some of my favorite moments of the season were the Soderbergh-styled heist episode that absolutely delivered on the idea of "Oceans 11 but with D&D antics" and an episode where the party visited Scanlan, letting Sam Riegel show off some fantastic range between the conflicting excitement and hurt of seeing his friends again, and a battle against a new bardic foe voiced by a friend of Critical Role, Tom Cardy.

The season is also peppered with incredible cameos and references, bringing back guests who played in the original Campaign 1 Actual Play, and other cameos teasing events to come… potentially in other Critical Role shows. It's clear that the writing team is not shying away from using anything from the additional decade of Critical Role lore to enhance this retelling of Vox Machina's story and treat the fans who have been ravenously consuming every aspect of Critical Role.

There are again more variations to the plot, especially as The Legend of Vox Machina serves to distance itself from some of the names that were directly used from the greater D&D lore, like never using Vecna's name, but these changes never feel like they diminish the story; rather, they tell the story as it makes most sense for an animated TV show instead of a D&D table where the party will traditionally stay together.

The Legend of Vox Machina Season 4 Review | Final Thoughts

With so much wrapping up after Season 3 there was a bit of a strange feeling going into Season 4 that the adventuring engine needed to be revved up again. I was a big fan of the slow approach to bringing the band back together, building up suspense and meaningful reveals, and a big conclusion that's left me already checking my watch for the next season's release.

The returning members of Vox Machina do start in self-isolation and doubt, making each feel a bit samey, but it's fantastic to see them relearn their ability to share and rely on one another through Wayne Brady's fantastic performance as Taryon Darrington.

As a longtime fan, the easter eggs absolutely blew me away this season. There's one moment especially, I'm waiting to break the fandom, and it can't come soon enough.

Season 4 in its complete form does feel like the first half of a season, but I can't wait for the payoff when The Legend of Vox Machina returns for its fifth and final season.

The Legend of Vox Machina Season 4 was reviewed with screeners provided by the publisher

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/reviews/legend-of-vox-machina-s4-review-family-divided
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Edge of Memories' Composer Wants to Hit the "Golden Age" of JRPG Music
item.content:encoded:
A JRPG-inspired game made by a French developer? You might immediately think of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, but there's a new one on the horizon. Edge of Memories, developed by Midgar Studio, is slated for a 2026 release, and it's tapping into any nostalgia you might have for JRPGs.

The love for this decades-old genre shows practically everywhere you look, from the game's art direction to the studio's name. Edge of Memories' Lead Composer Cédric Menendez "with no hesitation" lists Final Fantasy VI (and The Legend of Zelda: A Link to the Past) as major influences.

For Edge of Memories, he also worked with composer and arranger Mariam Abounnasr, who was previously an assistant to Yasunori Mitsuda on a previous project. If you don't know, Mitsuda-san composed music for Chrono Trigger and Xenoblade Chronicles. We spoke with Menendez and Abounnasr via email to get the scoop on what we can expect from Edge of Memories' musical identity.

What Is Edge of Memories?

Edge of Memories is a Japanese-inspired action RPG set on a fantasy continent named Avaris. A destructive affliction simply called the Corruption has already devastated another continent, and now it's spreading throughout Avaris.

This disease has forced many to be nomadic, including the main character Eline. In true main-character fashion, she has a mysterious dark power within her that could stop the Corruption, and it's up to her and her allies to scour Avaris, learn its secrets, and potentially save the world. If you want to try it yourself, there will be a free demo for Edge of Memories during Steam Next Fest starting June 15.

On the game's Steam page, Midgar Studio promises "an intense story with no dull moments," and if you see the videos of the combat in action, the gameplay looks promising, too. The studio also advertises "a poetic soundtrack," which I wanted to learn more about.

JRPGs and music go hand in hand, and I'm not just saying that because I've seen a few live concerts for video game music. Memories of and fondness for games are tied so closely to their melodies, and Edge of Memories has some pull that make it worth watching—including the singer for NieR: Automata.

From Edge of Eternity to Edge of Memories

Edge of Memories isn't the first game in this IP; Midgar shipped Edge of Eternity in 2018, and a lot has changed since. The team grew from five to 20 developers. Previously, Menendez handled music composition and sound design, but this time around, he has a team with two sound designers: Ocean Pauget and Hadrien Pellegrini.

"It’s actually the biggest lesson I learned from Edge of Eternity: you just can't do everything yourself," Menendez said. "If you're forced to, you must be smart about your time—learning how to adapt, reuse assets efficiently, and build generic systems to stay on track."

Another big change from Edge of Eternity that more people will likely notice is the total shift in gameplay style. Eternity featured turn-based combat, while Memories is going for real-time combat. This change of pace is, as Menendez put it, "a total gamechanger" when it comes to approaching the music.

"Rather than cutting from exploration to combat, I layer new instruments over the existing theme. Music shifts and builds intensity in real-time, staying cohesive," he said. "It’s a huge challenge from a compositional standpoint because you must manage all these harmonic and rhythmic constraints, but it creates a smooth, uninterrupted musical flow that follows the action perfectly."

As you might expect with a smaller team, a lot of the work happens in tandem as everyone moves toward the finish line. To create the music for Edge of Memories, Menendez works closely with the art director and narrative team. Their works influence each other, creating what he called "seamless symbiosis."

"As soon as the first environments take shape, I compose a piano 'block-out' based on our discussions with the art director," he said. "The artists then continue their work with that music in mind, which ensures a level of consistency that really drives immersion. On a systemic level, it also allows us to build complex audio systems alongside programmers and game designers from the get-go. It’s incredibly useful for getting effective feedback and, once again, enhancing the player’s immersion."

Paying Homage to Japan

Midgar Studio's affection for Japanese games should come as no surprise, if the name didn't give it away. Hover, the studio's first project, has some Jet Set Radio in its DNA—quite literally, considering composer Hideki Naganuma contributed to the soundtrack. For Edge of Eternity, Menendez worked with Yasunori Mitsuda, the composer of Chrono Trigger on some songs.

"Working with these composers was an absolute pleasure," Menendez said. "On projects like Edge of Eternity and Hover, we brought in guest composers quite early in the creative process, which actually allowed their work to influence the developers. On a personal level, it was a huge honor to compose alongside such world-class artists."

Edge of Eternity was also one of Mariam Abounnasr's first projects as an arranger and composer. She assisted Mitsuda-san during that time, helping him orchestrate some pieces. Now 10 years later, she's back with "far more confidence" (in her words) and four tracks of her own for Midgar's upcoming sequel.

"It all started when Mitsuda-san suggested I participate as a composer," she said. "Although each of the four tracks carries a different color and mood, they also have a firm melodic line, which brings out a melancholic feeling (especially the main theme) and expresses how it is to live in a world that is full of unsettling occurrences."

Mitsuda-san still contributed one song to Edge of Memories: its final song, which features vocalist Emi Evans. She's known for creating and singing the "chaos language" in NieR: Automata, and she lent her vocals more than just the final track in the game. This time around though, she's sticking to more widely understood languages, like English and Japanese.

"Just like Edge of Eternity, I wanted a modern soundtrack that still hits those 'golden age' JRPG notes, like Final Fantasy VI or VII," Menendez said. "But unlike Edge of Eternity, I really wanted the vocals to take center stage this time, similar to the style of NieR: Automata. So, I opted for a lighter orchestral arrangement to make sure the lead vocals could truly shine."

I'm looking forward to seeing more of Edge of Memories as it gets closer to release this year. It'll launch on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S, and there's a demo coming to Steam Next Fest on June 15.

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/interviews/edge-of-memories-composer-wants-to-hit-golden-age-of-jrpg-music
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
